title: VO countdown: How soon after treatment can you spot a vascular occlusion？
filename: VO countdown： How soon after treatment can you spot a vascular occlusion？.en.vtt
excerpt: So how soon after a treatment can you spot a vascular occlusion [Music] so this depends on many different variables first of all you've got to be looking for one so the number one tip i can give you is look for vascular occlusions after a procedure it is much easier to sleep bett…

segments.text: so how soon after a treatment can you spot a vascular occlusion [Music] so this depends on many different variables first of all you've got to be looking for one so the number one tip i can give you is look for vascular occlusions after a procedure it is much easier to sleep better at night if after every procedure you know that you've checked that blood flow is normal after you left that patient work on your capillary refill technique because a short tap on the skin is insufficient you need to squeeze the blood out of the skin so four seconds roughly of squeezing the blood out of the skin with a firm compress and then you need to release it and look carefully and get that timing right now for most cases it's really obvious the skin is normal and you make a note of that so in case the patient rings back you've at least got that bit of information that it was normal when you last saw them the big vast reclusions should be obvious on the moment of treatment so you once you've done the procedure if you do completely refill

test and it's a large vascular occlusion it should be immediately visible there is a theory that a small amount of filler could be in a vessel not cause a vascular occlusion because there are enough um collateral supplying blood to the tissue from other parts of the skin but then it may break off and block off a small section now they could come later but by definition that whole concept would imply that they are smaller so you're unlikely to block a very large area with one of the delayed vascular occlusions i think they're much rarer and they're much smaller so we really want to be getting it right with those immediate vascular occlusions of large occlusions where you've injected 0.1 you know 0.15 into one artery that should be very obvious as a problem they shouldn't leave your office until you've restored that blood flow and you should get that diagnosed immediately after the procedure not two or three hours later when they've come back with symptoms you
